translation: And after the exile to Babylon of Jeconiah, he begot Salathiel; and Salathiel begot Zorobabel.

alternate_translations.translation: And after the Babylonian removal of Jechoniah, Jechoniah fathered Salathiel; and Salathiel fathered Zorobabel.
alternate_translations.notes: μετοικεσίαν can mean “removal” or “deportation/exile,” and the genitive “Βαβυλῶνος” can be read as “to Babylon” (i.e., exile/removal that took him there). “Fathered” is a close rendering of ἐγέννησεν (“begot”).

alternate_translations.translation: After the deportation to Babylon of Jehoiachin (Jechoniah), he begot Salathiel, and Salathiel begot Zorobabel.
alternate_translations.notes: Ἰεχονίας is commonly rendered as Jeconiah/Jechoniah; in some contexts it corresponds to Jehoiachin, so this is a valid interpretive alternative when aligning names across traditions.

notes: This clause comes from a genealogical sequence (cf. Matthew 1:12). The Greek uses μετοικεσία (“relocation/deportation/exile”) with Βαβυλῶνος (“of/to Babylon”), so the sense is “after Jeconiah/Jechoniah was carried off to Babylon.” Proper names: Ἰεχονίας = Jeconiah/Jechoniah; Σαλαθιήλ = Salathiel; Ζοροβαβέλ = Zorobabel.
